figures.caption: Fig. 1: Experimental setup of the tendon-driven soft robotic manipulator used to validate the proposed closed-form CLF–CBF controller. The two-segment robot is mounted to a ceiling fixture and operates in an environment with spherical obstacles. Reflective markers are attached to each segment for real-time motion capture and feedback control during safe trajectory tracking experiments.
Fig. 8: Experimental results. Comparison of target and obstacle distances for closed-form CLF-CBF controller, and a pure QP CLF controller during the hardware experiment.

Fig. 7: Experimental results. Sequence of stills showing the system evolution under: a) the closed-form CLF–CBF controller, b) a QP CLF controller. The target position is highlighted in red, and the obstacle is outlined in green. Consistently, the closed-form CLF–CBF controller avoids the obstacle entirely, whereas the pure CLF controller reaches the target but violates the safety constraints.

Fig. 2: Simulation results. Circular trajectory tracking pattern in 2D. The trajectories represent the motion path of the soft robot tip center, while the red-shaded region indicates the collision area. The RMSE of each case is shown in the legend, consistently demonstrating that the closed-form controller achieves more accurate and smoother trajectories than the baseline QP controller.

Fig. 3: Quantitative evaluation of body discretization and controller efficiency. (a) Increasing Nres improves geometric fidelity, with rapidly diminishing Hausdorff error after ∼ 400 spheres. (b) The closed-form CLF–CBF solver maintains low computation time even at high discretization resolutions, in contrast to the generic QP solver.
